items.back: 1. Fly at appropriate VFR altitude. 2. Comply with VFR visibility and cloud distance criteria. 3. See and avoid other aircraft. 4. Comply with IFR rules applicable to the flight (e.g., adhere to ATC clearances).

items.front: VFR-on-top: ATC services that continue per 7-3-1 note 2
items.back: IFR separation is NOT applied, but controllers MUST continue: traffic advisories, safety alerts, and merging target procedures.

1. Pilot requests. 2. Inform of reported tops OR no tops report available. 3. When necessary, issue alternative clearance for separation. 4. Reclear to MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP when pilot reports reaching it. Phraseology: CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP. IF NOT ON TOP AT (altitude), MAINTAIN (altitude), AND ADVISE.

Do NOT use VFR-on-top between sunset and sunrise to separate holding aircraft from each other or from en route aircraft UNLESS altitude restrictions are applied for IFR vertical separation. Use: MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP AT OR ABOVE/BELOW/BETWEEN (altitudes).

VFR-on-top: IFR flight plan remains, IFR separation suspended, pilot selects VFR altitude per 91.159, pilot complies with both VFR and IFR rules. VFR conditions: clearance ends at fix/time, ATC specifies altitudes, used only for pilot-requested climb/descent or terminal noise abatement.

items.front: VFR-on-top cruising levels per 7-3-2 and 14 CFR 91.159(a)
items.back: Above 3,000 ft AGL to FL 180: Magnetic courses 0-179 = ODD altitudes + 500 ft (e.g., 3,500, 5,500). Magnetic courses 180-359 = EVEN altitudes + 500 ft (e.g., 4,500, 8,500). Exception: aircraft in holding pattern of 2 min or less, or turning.

Inform VFR-on-top aircraft when a report indicates non-compliance with 14 CFR 91.159(a). Phraseology: VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE: ODD/EVEN ALTITUDES/FLIGHT LEVELS PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.
